    "text": "**Status: lib changes complete, API docs + changelog still pending.**\n\n**Done in the library (`watercolor-lib.js`):**\n\n1. **Brush dynamics state** \u2014 `_brushPressure`, `_brushFlow`, `_usePointerPressure`, plus `_strokeStrength` captured at pointer-down so a stroke has consistent strength.\n\n2. **Pointer handlers updated** \u2014 pointer-down reads `e.pressure` (when `usePointerPressure` on) and stores per-stroke strength; `applyPendingPaint` uses `_brushFlow` for spacing and `_strokeStrength` instead of the hardcoded 0.4 and 0.7.\n\n3. **SVG trace now uses the active brush** \u2014 pigment, brushSize, and pressure are read from current state by default; `opts` parameter lets callers override per-call.\n\n4. **SVG `flipY` option, default true** \u2014 fixes the upside-down trace. Note: the original v0.26.2 had identical Y math, so this either was always flipped or your SVG sources use Y-up convention. Defaulting to `flipY: true` makes the common observed behavior correct; opt out with `flipY: false`.\n\n5. **Shared `_resolvePigmentOption` helper** \u2014 handles `'rose' | 'yellow' | 'blue' | 'cerulean' | 'rainbow' | 'water' | 'lift' | 'mask'` strings.\n\n6. **New API methods** (verified in stubbed test):\n   - `pressure(v)` \u2014 0..1 stamp strength, clamped\n   - `flow(v)` \u2014 0.05..2.0 stamp spacing, clamped\n   - `usePointerPressure(v)` \u2014 toggle stylus pressure response\n   - `continuousFlow(v)` \u2014 moved to its proper home (deduplicated, was declared twice)\n\n**Still pending \u2014 will need a follow-up turn:**\n\n- Updating the API docs section in the HTML to document `pressure()`, `flow()`, `usePointerPressure()`, `continuousFlow()`, and the new `traceSVG(svgText, opts)` signature with `flipY`\n- Changelog entry for v0.29 (or extending v0.28 \u2014 the lib has v0.29 markers in comments but the version label still says v0.28)\n- Rebuilding and shipping the single-file HTML\n\nLet me know if you want me to continue with the docs + changelog + ship \u2014 that's the natural next step.",

Before v0.29, holding still after the initial dab produced nothing further. With this on, holding the brush builds a growing puddle that bleeds outward through the simulation's diffusion. Works for all brush types: pigments add color + water, water adds water + lifts, lift draws pigment back into suspension continuously, rainbow drips through its hue cycle, mask paints freezing zones the longer you hold.</li>\n  2055\t          <li>Per-frame deposit strength is <code>0.12</code> &mdash; about <code>7&times;</code> weaker than a single stamp (<code>0.7</code>), tuned so ~1 second of holding accumulates a similar amount of pigment to a deliberate per-stamp dab. The constant <code>CONTINUOUS_FLOW_STRENGTH</code> in the lib's POINTER PAINTING block is the single source of truth if you want to retune.</li>\n  2056\t          <li>New toggle in the toggles row: <strong>Continuous flow</strong>. Zero overhead while idle, one <code>paintAt</code> call per held-still frame otherwise &mdash; same cost as a single stamp during a drag.</li>\n  2059\t        </ul>\n  2060\t\n  2061\t        <!-- ===== v0.28 ===== -->\n  2062\t        <h2 id=\"cl-0-28\">v0.28 &mdash; Mobile mode + palm rejection</h2>\n  2063\t        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n  2064\t          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Mobile mode.</strong> New toggle in the toggles row plus a library option <code>{ mobile: true }</code> on <code>Watercolor.create()</code>. Auto-enabled on touch-primary devices via <code>matchMedia('(pointer: coarse)')</code> detection at create time; auto-detection result is exposed at <code>inst.mobileDetected()</code> while the live state is <code>inst.mobile()</code>. The toggle is independent of detection, so users on desktop with a touchscreen can opt in, and users on a tablet can opt out.</li>\n  2065\t          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Long touch strokes.</strong> Already worked at the pointer-event level (the per-frame <code>applyPendingPaint()</code> interpolates from last-known to current with stamp spacing of <code>r &times; 0.4</code>, producing continuous strokes for any drag speed including fast finger swipes). v0.28 ensures touch events get through reliably by handling palm rejection upstream.</li>\n  2066\t          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Palm rejection (three heuristics, combined).</strong> Active only when mobile mode is on; mouse and pen-only flows are untouched.\n  2067\t            <ol>\n  2068\t              <li><strong>Contact size.</strong> <code>PointerEvent.width</code> and <code>height</code> give the contact area in CSS pixels. Touches wider than <code>PALM_CONTACT_PX = 40</code> in either dimension are dropped. (Many browsers report 1&times;1 for touches with no real area information; those bypass this filter to avoid false rejections.)</li>\n  2069\t              <li><strong>Pen lockout.</strong> Once <code>_activePointerType === 'pen'</code> latches, any concurrent <code>pointerType === 'touch'</code> events are dropped. This is the standard iPad-with-Apple-Pencil pattern: the palm rests on the screen while the stylus draws.</li>\n  2070\t              <li><strong>First-touch-wins.</strong> When painting starts on <code>pointerId X</code>, the lib remembers X and ignores additional pointer events with different IDs until X ends. Filters multi-touch palm landings on phones, where the palm lands a fraction of a second after the finger.</li>\n  2071\t            </ol>\n  2072\t          </li>\n  2073\t          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Mobile-aware UI.</strong> When mobile mode is on, the floating cursor preview is hidden (you can see your finger; the preview was designed for mouse hover) and the body picks up a <code>wc-mobile</code> class so any host CSS can react. On auto-detection, the control panel comes up collapsed to give the canvas more room \u2014 tap the toggle in the corner to expand it.</li>\n  2074\t          <li>API additions: <code>inst.mobile()</code> / <code>inst.mobile(true|false)</code> getter-setter, <code>inst.mobileDetected()</code> for the one-time detection snapshot.</li>\n  2075\t        </ul>\n  2076\t\n  2077\t        <!-- ===== v0.27 ===== -->\n  2078\t        <h2 id=\"cl-0-27\">v0.27 &mdash; Library extraction + UI on top</h2>\n  2079\t        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n  2080\t          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> The simulation core is now a standalone JavaScript library, <code>watercolor-lib.js</code>. This page (v0.27) is the same HTML/CSS as v0.26.2, but the inline ~5,800-line <code>&lt;script&gt;</code> block is replaced by <code>&lt;script src=\"watercolor-lib.js\"&gt;&lt;/script&gt;</code> + a ~390-line wiring script that hooks each UI element to a method on the library instance. State isolation is via JavaScript closure semantics &mdash; every variable inside the factory's <code>create()</code> call is unique per instance.</li>\n[8074 lines total]",

      "new_str": "        <!-- ===== v0.29 ===== -->\n        <h2 id=\"cl-0-29\">v0.29 &mdash; Brush dynamics + SVG fixes</h2>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-fix\">fix</span> <strong>SVG tracing painted upside-down.</strong> Many SVG sources &mdash; CAD exports, plot-style diagrams, scientific figures, and files originally drawn in Y-up Cartesian convention &mdash; produce inverted output through the standard SVG Y-down spec when traced. Added a <code>flipY</code> option to <code>traceSVG()</code> defaulting to <code>true</code>: <code>y = offsetY + (vbH - (pt.y - vbY)) &times; s</code>. Opt out with <code>traceSVG(svg, { flipY: false })</code> if your SVG is in standard Y-down convention.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-fix\">fix</span> <strong>SVG trace used hardcoded constants.</strong> The pre-v0.29 implementation traced with <code>SVG_TRACE_BRUSH_PX = 9</code> and <code>SVG_TRACE_STRENGTH = 0.45</code> regardless of what brush you had selected in the panel. Now reads <code>currentPigment</code>, <code>brushSize</code>, and <code>pressure()</code> from live state at trace time, so the same SVG paints differently depending on your active brush settings &mdash; same way the mouse brush does.</li>\n\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Continuous flow.</strong> When you press and hold a pointer in place, pigment and water now keep flowing into the wet area each frame &mdash; like a marker or wet brush pressed against paper. Before v0.29, holding still after the initial dab produced nothing further. Works for all brush types: pigments add color + water, water adds water + lifts, lift draws pigment back into suspension continuously, rainbow drips through its hue cycle, mask paints freezing zones the longer you hold. Per-frame deposit strength is <code>CONTINUOUS_FLOW_STRENGTH = 0.12</code> &mdash; about <code>7&times;</code> weaker than a single stamp.</li>\n\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Brush dynamics exposed as API.</strong> Three previously-hardcoded constants in the pointer-event handlers and SVG tracer are now first-class settings:\n            <ul>\n              <li><code>pressure(v)</code> &mdash; base stamp strength, range 0..1, default 0.7. Was hardcoded as the <code>0.7</code> in every <code>paintAt(..., 0.7)</code> call. Lower values produce lighter strokes; higher saturates faster.</li>\n              <li><code>flow(v)</code> &mdash; stamp spacing along a drag, as a fraction of brush radius. Range 0.05..2.0, default 0.4. Was hardcoded as <code>r &times; 0.4</code>. Smaller = more stamps = smoother continuous strokes (and more sim work). Larger = fewer stamps = dotted/dabbed look.</li>\n              <li><code>usePointerPressure(v)</code> &mdash; when true, multiplies stamp strength by <code>PointerEvent.pressure</code>. Useful for stylus + tablet users. Default false because mouse browsers commonly report a neutral <code>0.5</code> that would create unwanted variability. Captured at pointer-down and held for the rest of the stroke for consistency.</li>\n              <li><code>continuousFlow(v)</code> &mdash; getter-setter for the continuous-flow toggle. Default true. Accepts <code>{ continuousFlow: false }</code> in create options to start off.</li>\n            </ul>\n          </li>\n\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>SVG trace accepts per-call overrides.</strong> The full signature is now <code>traceSVG(svgText, opts?)</code> where <code>opts</code> is <code>{ pigment, brushSize, strength, flipY }</code>. Each option falls through to live state if not specified, so <code>traceSVG(svg)</code> still uses the active brush. Pigment accepts a name string (<code>'rose'</code>, <code>'yellow'</code>, <code>'blue'</code>, <code>'cerulean'</code>, <code>'rainbow'</code>, <code>'water'</code>, <code>'lift'</code>, <code>'mask'</code>) or numeric index.</li>\n\n          <li>Implementation: introduced <code>_resolvePigmentOption()</code> shared between <code>traceSVG</code>, <code>paintText</code>, and any future API surface that accepts named pigments. <code>_strokeStrength</code> captured at pointer-down so a stroke has consistent feel even when <code>usePointerPressure</code> is on and individual move events report varying <code>e.pressure</code> values.</li>\n        </ul>\n\n        <!-- ===== v0.28 ===== -->",

Default: <code>pressure()</code>.</dd>\n          <dt><code>flipY</code></dt>\n          <dd>Mirror Y around the SVG's vertical center. Default <code>true</code>. Fixes the common upside-down result from Y-up SVG sources (CAD exports, scientific plots, files originally drawn in Cartesian convention). Pass <code>false</code> if your SVG is in standard Y-down convention.</dd>\n        </dl>\n\n        <h2 id=\"api-brush-dynamics\">Brush dynamics</h2>\n        <p>\n          Three knobs that apply equally to mouse, pen, finger, and\n          SVG tracing. Previously hardcoded in the pointer handlers.\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\"><span class=\"docs-sig\">Watercolor.pressure()</span> \u2192 current value\n<span class=\"docs-sig\">Watercolor.pressure(v)</span> // 0..1, default 0.7\n\n<span class=\"docs-sig\">Watercolor.flow()</span> \u2192 current value\n<span class=\"docs-sig\">Watercolor.flow(v)</span> // 0.05..2.0, default 0.4\n\n<span class=\"docs-sig\">Watercolor.usePointerPressure(v)</span> // default false\n\n<span class=\"docs-sig\">Watercolor.continuousFlow(v)</span> // default true</pre>\n        <dl class=\"docs-deflist\">\n          <dt><code>pressure</code></dt>\n          <dd>Base stamp strength for each dab. Lower values produce lighter strokes; higher saturate faster. Captured at pointer-down and held for the rest of the stroke so feel is consistent across a single drag.</dd>\n          <dt><code>flow</code></dt>\n          <dd>Stamp spacing along a drag, as a fraction of brush radius. Smaller = more stamps per stroke = smoother continuous look (and more sim work). Larger = fewer stamps = dotted/dabbed look. Clamped to <code>0.05..2.0</code> to prevent pathological values.</dd>\n          <dt><code>usePointerPressure</code></dt>\n          <dd>When true, multiplies stamp strength by <code>PointerEvent.pressure</code> from the stylus or pressure-sensitive trackpad. Default off because mouse browsers report a neutral <code>0.5</code> that would create unwanted dimming. Only consult <code>e.pressure</code> when this is on.</dd>\n          <dt><code>continuousFlow</code></dt>\n          <dd>When on, holding the pointer in place keeps pumping pigment + water at a reduced rate (<code>CONTINUOUS_FLOW_STRENGTH = 0.12</code>). Mimics how a real brush bleeds when held still on wet paper. Off = classic one-stamp-per-position behavior.</dd>\n        </dl>\n\n        <h2 id=\"api-toggles\">Toggles</h2>",
      "old_str": "        <pre class=\"docs-code\"><span class=\"docs-sig\">await Watercolor.traceSVG(source)</span> \u2192 points queued\n\n<span class=\"docs-sig\">Watercolor.cancelSVGTrace()</span></pre>\n        <p>\n          <code>source</code> accepts SVG markup (a string starting\n          with <code>&lt;</code>), a URL string (fetched then parsed),\n          or a <code>File</code> object (e.g. from a file picker).\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"api-toggles\">Toggles</h2>",

Applies evaporate's dry-out rule\n// unconditionally to every cell \u2014 all suspended pigment settles to\n// deposited, water and flow zero out. After this, all cells are below\n// the simulation's wet-threshold so the sim will idle out within a\n// frame. We render explicitly so the damp-sheen disappears from\n// unpainted cells immediately, without needing to wake the sim for 60\n// grace frames just to flush a visual that won't change again.\n//\n// v2 lib note: in v0.26.2 this also called updateBars() to refresh the\n// dryness/pigment indicator at the bottom of the panel. That UI is now\n// a host concern \u2014 the wiring polls state() every 250ms. We dispatch a\n// 'driedinstantly' event so a host that wants an immediate update\n// (without waiting for the next poll) can listen.\nfunction dryPaper() {\n  for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {\n    // Masked cells: frozen. Dry button can't reach them.\n    if (maskActive && mask[i] > MASK_THRESHOLD) continue;\n    // Settle suspended \u2192 deposited (same rule evaporate uses)\n    for (let k = 0; k < 3; k++) {\n      let nd = d[k][i] + g[k][i];\n      if (nd > MAX_PIGMENT) nd = MAX_PIGMENT;\n      d[k][i] = nd;\n      g[k][i] = 0;\n    }\n    wet[i] = 0;\n    u[i] = 0; v[i] = 0;\n    pressure[i] = 0;\n  }\n  // No wet, no suspended pigment, no pressure \u2192 no dynamics anywhere.\n  setActiveRectEmpty();\n  render();\n  targetEl.dispatchEvent(new CustomEvent('driedinstantly'));\n}",

The wiring already polls `wc.state()` every 250ms (via `setInterval(updateReadouts, 250)`), so the bars refresh on their own within 250ms of pressing Dry.\n\nFor instant feedback (no 250ms wait), I added a `'driedinstantly'` CustomEvent that the lib dispatches on `targetEl` after the dry pass completes. The wiring now listens for it:\n\n```js\nhost.addEventListener('driedinstantly', updateReadouts);\n```\n\nPressing Dry triggers an immediate readout refresh, while the regular 250ms poll continues to catch all other state changes.\n\n**I also did an orphan audit** of both files to catch any similar dangling references. Used a regex that:\n- Strips block comments, line comments, and string literals first (so words inside docstrings don't show up)\n- Finds all `identifier(` calls **not** preceded by `.` (so `el.foo()` method calls don't count)\n- Subtracts declarations, keywords, and global/built-in names\n\n**In the lib:** only `updateBars` came back as a genuine orphan (the rest were DOM method call false positives from my regex not catching every preceding `.`).\n\n**In the wiring:** four hits \u2014 `apiFn`, `fmt`, `fn`, `getter`, `setter` \u2014 all local function parameter names, not orphans.\n\nSo this was the only dangling reference. With those constraints in mind, I'd want to include practical recipes like painting a single dot, drawing continuous strokes in a loop, handling the paint-wait-dry cycle, setting up multiple instances, and tracing programmatic SVG paths.\n\nI should also flag the gotchas\u2014pigments mix subtractively without alpha, brush pressure is captured at pointer-down and won't update mid-stroke, masks stick until explicitly removed, resolution changes reset the canvas, and the active rectangle optimization can behave unexpectedly on small canvases.       "new_str": "      </section>\n\n      <!-- ============================== AGENTS ============================== -->\n      <section class=\"docs-page\" data-section=\"agents\">\n        <h1 id=\"agents\">Agents &amp; automation</h1>\n        <p class=\"docs-lede\">\n          This page is for coding agents and automation tools &mdash;\n          Cursor, Claude Code, ChatGPT, Antigravity, Windsurf,\n          Continue, Cline, Aider, custom scripts, evaluation\n          harnesses &mdash; that want to drive the canvas\n          programmatically. Humans are welcome too. The information\n          here is dense by design.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"agents-quick-spec\">Quick spec</h2>\n        <p>\n          <strong>What this is.</strong> A real-time pigment-and-water\n          simulation, written in vanilla JavaScript with no\n          dependencies. The library implements the Curtis et al.\n          (1997) watercolor model with Kubelka&ndash;Munk compositing,\n          shallow-water flow on a staggered grid (Foster &amp;\n          Metaxas 1996), and edge darkening. Runs CPU-side by default\n          with an optional WebGL2 render path for compositing.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          <strong>How to mount.</strong> The library exposes a\n          factory:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">const wc = Watercolor.create(targetEl, options?);</pre>\n        <p>\n          Every method on the returned <code>wc</code> handle is\n          stateful and synchronous. There are no callbacks, no\n          Promises (except <code>traceSVG</code> when passed a URL),\n          and no event-loop coordination required from the caller.\n          The simulation runs on a single\n          <code>requestAnimationFrame</code> loop owned by the\n          instance. The host's only job is to call methods when\n          state changes are wanted.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          <strong>Coordinate system.</strong> All position arguments\n          (<code>paintAt</code>, <code>paintText</code>, cursor\n          positions) are in <em>grid coordinates</em>: integer cells\n          on the simulation grid, not display pixels. Read\n          <code>wc.grid.width</code> /\n          <code>wc.grid.height</code> for current dimensions. Convert\n          display-pixel to grid via <code>wc.toGrid(clientX,\n          clientY)</code>.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          <strong>Idle behavior.</strong> The sim auto-idles when no\n          pigment is moving (no recent paint events, no animation\n          running, no visualization). When idle, the RAF loop is\n          essentially free &mdash; it ticks at 60Hz but skips\n          <code>simStep</code> and <code>render</code>. Painting via\n          <code>paintAt</code> automatically wakes the sim.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"agents-signatures\">Type signatures</h2>\n        <p>\n          TypeScript-style signatures for every public method.\n          Getter-setter pattern: omit the argument to read; pass to\n          write. All setters return the API handle (chainable) unless\n          otherwise noted.\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">// === FACTORY ===\nWatercolor.create(targetEl: HTMLElement, options?: CreateOptions): Instance\n\ninterface CreateOptions {\n  canvasScale?: number       // 0.1..1.0, default auto from target width\n  mobile?: boolean           // override matchMedia('(pointer: coarse)')\n  continuousFlow?: boolean   // default true\n  pointer?: boolean          // default true; false disables pointer events\n}\n\n// === BRUSH STATE ===\nbrushSize(): number | brushSize(px: number): Instance     // display pixels\npigment(): number | pigment(name: string | idx: number): Instance\npressure(): number | pressure(v: number): Instance        // 0..1, default 0.7\nflow(): number | flow(v: number): Instance                // 0.05..2.0, default 0.4\npaintLoad(): number | paintLoad(v: number): Instance      // 0.1+, default 1.0\nwaterLoad(): number | waterLoad(v: number): Instance      // 0.2+, default 1.0\ncontinuousFlow(): boolean | continuousFlow(v: boolean): Instance\nusePointerPressure(): boolean | usePointerPressure(v: boolean): Instance\n\n// === PAINTING ===\npaintAt(gx: number, gy: number, gridRadius: number,\n        pigment: number | string, strength: number): Instance\npaintText(text: string, opts?: TextOptions): number   // returns dabs painted\ntraceSVG(svgText: string, opts?: SVGTraceOptions): number  // returns points queued\ncancelSVGTrace(): Instance\n\ninterface TextOptions {\n  x?: number; y?: number; fontSize?: number; sampleStep?: number;\n  strength?: number; pigment?: string | number;\n}\ninterface SVGTraceOptions {\n  pigment?: string | number; brushSize?: number;\n  strength?: number; flipY?: boolean;   // flipY default true\n}\n\n// === SCENES ===\nsetAnimation(name: AnimationName, opts?: object): Instance\ngetAnimation(): string\nsetVisualization(name: VisualizationName, opts?: object): Instance\ngetVisualization(): string\nsetBackground(name: BackgroundName): Instance\nisBackgroundRunning(): boolean\n\ntype AnimationName = 'off' | 'ai' | 'rainy' | 'sunny' | 'windy'\n                   | 'thunderstorm' | 'partlyCloudy' | 'snowing'\n                   | 'snowingAdditive' | 'tornado';\ntype VisualizationName = 'off' | 'kaleidoscope' | 'lissajous'\n                       | 'flowField' | 'pulse';\ntype BackgroundName = 'day' | 'dawn' | 'sunset' | 'night' | 'tornado';\n\n// === TOGGLES ===\nedgeDarkening(v?: boolean): boolean | Instance\npauseDrying(v?: boolean): boolean | Instance\nautoDryBackground(v?: boolean): boolean | Instance\nfadePainting(v?: boolean): boolean | Instance\ntransparent(v?: boolean): boolean | Instance\nwebgl(v?: boolean): boolean | Instance\nwebglAvailable(): boolean\nwebglDebugTint(v?: boolean): boolean | Instance\nmobile(v?: boolean): boolean | Instance\nmobileDetected(): boolean\nperf(v?: boolean): boolean | Instance\n\n// === CONTINUOUS PARAMS ===\nfadeHalfLife(ms?: number): number | Instance    // 500..30000\nscale(v?: number): number | Instance            // 1..6, REBUILDS sim\npaperWetness(name: string): Instance\npaperColor(r?: number, g?: number, b?: number): object | Instance\n\n// === ACTIONS ===\nrewet(): Instance        // re-saturate paper\ndry(): Instance          // instant-dry; fires 'driedinstantly' event on targetEl\nreset(): Instance        // clear all paint + paper state\nremoveMask(): Instance\nsplash(arg1?, arg2?): Instance\nsplashPresets(): string[]\npaperWetnessPresets(): string[]\npigments(): { index: number, name: string }[]\n\n// === RENDER + EXPORT ===\nexportPNG(opts?: { mimeType?: string }): string  // returns data URL\nbuildPigmentSwatches(rootEl: HTMLElement): Instance\n\n// === STATE ===\nstate(): StateSnapshot\ngrid: { width: number, height: number }\ntoGrid(clientX: number, clientY: number): { gx: number, gy: number }\nperfMetrics(): PerfSnapshot\ndestroy(): void   // cancels RAF, removes listeners, drops references</pre>\n\n        <p>\n          <strong>Pigment name resolution.</strong> All methods\n          accepting a pigment accept one of:\n          <code>'rose'</code> | <code>0</code>,\n          <code>'yellow'</code> | <code>1</code>,\n          <code>'blue'</code> | <code>'cerulean'</code> | <code>2</code>,\n          <code>'water'</code> | <code>-1</code>,\n          <code>'lift'</code> | <code>-2</code>,\n          <code>'rainbow'</code> | <code>-3</code>,\n          <code>'mask'</code> | <code>-4</code>.\n          Unknown names throw <code>Error: Unknown pigment: &lt;name&gt;</code>.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"agents-recipes\">Recipes</h2>\n        <p>\n          Common tasks as standalone snippets. All assume\n          <code>wc = Watercolor.create(targetEl)</code> has been\n          called and the instance is in its default state.\n        </p>\n\n        <h3>Single dab at canvas center</h3>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">const { width, height } = wc.grid;\nwc.paintAt(width / 2, height / 2, 20, 'blue', 0.7);</pre>\n\n        <h3>Continuous stroke from A to B</h3>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">// Interpolated stamps along the line. Spacing should be roughly\n// brush-radius / 4 for smoothness. paintAt internally takes a\n// grid-radius (not diameter), so divide by 2.\nfunction stroke(x1, y1, x2, y2, brushPx = 20, pigment = 'rose') {\n  const r = brushPx / 2;\n  const dx = x2 - x1, dy = y2 - y1;\n  const dist = Math.sqrt(dx * dx + dy * dy);\n  const steps = Math.max(1, Math.ceil(dist / (r * 0.4)));\n  for (let i = 0; i &lt;= steps; i++) {\n    const t = i / steps;\n    wc.paintAt(x1 + dx * t, y1 + dy * t, r, pigment, 0.7);\n  }\n}\nstroke(100, 100, 500, 400);</pre>\n\n        <h3>Loop-driven painting (timed sequence)</h3>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">// Paint a sequence with realistic timing: paint, let it bleed for\n// 2 seconds, paint again, let it dry. Note that you do NOT need to\n// step the sim manually \u2014 the lib's RAF runs continuously. You\n// just decide when to inject paint events.\nasync function sequence() {\n  const sleep = ms =&gt; new Promise(r =&gt; setTimeout(r, ms));\n  wc.pigment('rose'); wc.brushSize(40);\n  wc.paintAt(300, 300, 20, 'rose', 0.7);\n  await sleep(2000);                          // bleed for 2s\n  wc.pigment('blue');\n  wc.paintAt(400, 350, 18, 'blue', 0.7);     // wet-on-wet mixing\n  await sleep(3000);                          // bleed more\n  wc.dry();                                   // instant dry\n  const png = wc.exportPNG();\n  return png;\n}</pre>\n\n        <h3>Multi-instance setup</h3>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">// Each create() returns an independent simulation. No globals\n// are shared between instances; safe to mount many on one page.\nconst instA = Watercolor.create(document.getElementById('canvas-a'));\nconst instB = Watercolor.create(document.getElementById('canvas-b'),\n  { canvasScale: 0.5 });   // half-resolution for the smaller embed\ninstA.setBackground('sunset');\ninstB.setAnimation('rainy');</pre>\n\n        <h3>SVG trace with custom brush</h3>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">const svgText = await fetch('/some-vector.svg').then(r =&gt; r.text());\nwc.traceSVG(svgText, {\n  pigment: 'cerulean',\n  brushSize: 30,\n  strength: 0.6,\n  flipY: true   // default; set false for standard Y-down SVGs\n});</pre>\n\n        <h3>Set scene + animation, then capture frames</h3>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">// Useful for procedurally generating training data / examples.\nwc.setBackground('dawn');\nwc.setAnimation('rainy');\nconst frames = [];\nconst intervalId = setInterval(() =&gt; {\n  frames.push(wc.exportPNG());\n  if (frames.length &gt;= 60) {\n    clearInterval(intervalId);\n    wc.setAnimation('off');\n  }\n}, 1000 / 30);   // 30 fps capture</pre>\n\n        <h3>Listen for sim state changes</h3>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">// The library dispatches CustomEvents on targetEl for some\n// state changes. Currently:\n//   'rescaled'         \u2014 fired after scale() rebuilds the sim\n//   'driedinstantly'   \u2014 fired after dry() completes\n//   'pigmentchange'    \u2014 fired by lib-built swatches when clicked\n//                        (only when using buildPigmentSwatches)\ntargetEl.addEventListener('rescaled', (e) =&gt; {\n  console.log('grid is now', e.detail.gridWidth, 'x', e.detail.gridHeight);\n});</pre>\n\n        <h2 id=\"agents-invariants\">Gotchas &amp; invariants</h2>\n        <p>\n          Surprising-but-true facts. These are things that will save\n          you from chasing phantom bugs.\n        </p>\n        <dl class=\"docs-deflist\">\n          <dt>Coordinates are grid units, NOT pixels.</dt>\n          <dd>\n            <code>paintAt(100, 100, 20, 'blue', 0.7)</code> paints at\n            grid cell (100, 100) with a grid-radius of 20 cells. With\n            the default <code>SCALE = 1.75</code>, those grid units\n            map to about 175 display pixels from the top-left. Use\n            <code>wc.grid.width</code> / <code>wc.grid.height</code>\n            for proportional placement; use <code>wc.toGrid()</code>\n            to convert clientX/Y from mouse events.\n          </dd>\n\n          <dt>paintAt takes grid-RADIUS, not diameter.</dt>\n          <dd>\n            <code>brushSize()</code> is in display pixels and is\n            diameter. When converting between them: <code>r =\n            (brushSize / 2) / SCALE</code>. The recipe above does this\n            inline.\n          </dd>\n\n          <dt>Pigments mix subtractively, not RGB-additively.</dt>\n          <dd>\n            Red + green = brown-ish (not yellow), red + blue =\n            purple, etc. Driven by Kubelka&ndash;Munk K/S triplets\n            from Figure 5 of Curtis et al. 1997. <code>'water'</code>\n            doesn't deposit pigment; it deposits wetness and dilutes\n            whatever is there. <code>'lift'</code> draws pigment back\n            into suspension.\n          </dd>\n\n          <dt>scale() rebuilds the sim and clears the canvas.</dt>\n          <dd>\n            Changing resolution reallocates all state arrays. The\n            instance dispatches a <code>'rescaled'</code> event on\n            <code>targetEl</code> when done. Don't call\n            <code>scale()</code> in a tight loop &mdash; debounce by\n            ~80ms.\n          </dd>\n\n          <dt>mask is sticky.</dt>\n          <dd>\n            Mask deposits frozen-pigment-protection cells. Painting\n            with <code>'mask'</code> over an area makes that area\n            immutable to subsequent painting + diffusion until you\n            call <code>removeMask()</code>. <code>reset()</code> also\n            clears the mask along with everything else.\n          </dd>\n\n          <dt>WebGL toggle does not change physics.</dt>\n          <dd>\n            <code>wc.webgl(true)</code> moves the per-pixel\n            Kubelka&ndash;Munk compositing onto the GPU. The bleeding,\n            advection, evaporation, edge darkening &mdash; the actual\n            simulation &mdash; still runs on the CPU. So toggling\n            WebGL changes frame rate, not the resulting painting.\n          </dd>\n\n          <dt>fadePainting() doesn't fade unpainted pixels.</dt>\n          <dd>\n            Only cells with deposited pigment fade. The paper texture\n            itself doesn't decay. If a canvas is fully covered, fade\n            will gradually return everything to paper-tone; if it's\n            partially covered, only the painted regions thin out.\n          </dd>\n\n          <dt>destroy() is required for clean unmount.</dt>\n          <dd>\n            Without it, the RAF loop keeps running and the canvas\n            element keeps capturing pointer events. In test harnesses\n            or hot-reload scenarios, always <code>wc.destroy()</code>\n            before unmounting <code>targetEl</code>.\n          </dd>\n\n          <dt>SVG trace flipY default is true.</dt>\n          <dd>\n            Most SVG sources Y-down per spec, but many CAD/scientific\n            sources are Y-up. The default <code>flipY: true</code>\n            covers the common observed case. Pass\n            <code>{ flipY: false }</code> for standard Y-down sources.\n          </dd>\n\n          <dt>pressure() is captured at pointer-down.</dt>\n          <dd>\n            Changing <code>pressure()</code> mid-stroke does NOT\n            affect the in-progress drag. The strength for the rest\n            of the stroke is locked at pointer-down time. This is\n            intentional &mdash; per-event pressure variability looks\n            chaotic to most users.\n          </dd>\n        </dl>\n\n        <h2 id=\"agents-loop\">Driving from a loop</h2>\n        <p>\n          Patterns for autonomous canvas driving. The library is\n          designed to be controlled imperatively: you don't queue\n          events, you just call methods when you want changes.\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">// Pattern 1: Step-by-step deterministic sequence\nasync function paint() {\n  for (const step of plan) {\n    if (step.type === 'paint') {\n      wc.paintAt(step.x, step.y, step.r, step.pigment, step.strength);\n    } else if (step.type === 'wait') {\n      await new Promise(r =&gt; setTimeout(r, step.ms));\n    } else if (step.type === 'dry') {\n      wc.dry();\n    }\n  }\n}\n\n// Pattern 2: Continuous control loop with state polling\nfunction controller(targetX, targetY) {\n  let active = true;\n  function tick() {\n    if (!active) return;\n    const s = wc.state();\n    // Decide next action based on current state. For example, paint\n    // while the canvas is \"dry enough\" to absorb new paint cleanly.\n    if (!s.dryingPaused) {\n      wc.paintAt(targetX, targetY, 15, 'rose', 0.5);\n    }\n    requestAnimationFrame(tick);\n  }\n  tick();\n  return () =&gt; { active = false; };\n}\n\n// Pattern 3: Test harness \u2014 discrete steps with deterministic state\nasync function runEval(scenario) {\n  const host = document.createElement('div');\n  document.body.appendChild(host);\n  const wc = Watercolor.create(host, { pointer: false });   // no UI input\n  for (const action of scenario.actions) action(wc);\n  await new Promise(r =&gt; setTimeout(r, scenario.settleMs ?? 1500));\n  const png = wc.exportPNG();\n  wc.destroy();\n  document.body.removeChild(host);\n  return png;\n}</pre>\n        <p>\n          <strong>Determinism caveat.</strong> The sim isn't\n          bit-deterministic across runs &mdash; floating-point\n          ordering, RAF cadence, and the active-rect optimization\n          mean the same input sequence may produce slightly different\n          output. For deterministic evals, render frames yourself by\n          calling internal step methods directly via a fork, or\n          compare results structurally (pigment count, bounding box)\n          rather than byte-for-byte.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          <strong>Pointer-free instances.</strong> Pass\n          <code>{ pointer: false }</code> in create options to disable\n          the built-in pointer event handlers. Useful for test\n          harnesses where mouse-driven painting would interfere with\n          programmatic painting via <code>paintAt</code>.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"agents-llms-txt\">llms.txt &amp; AGENTS.md</h2>\n        <p>\n          Two emerging conventions for making projects readable by\n          coding agents. Neither is required to use this library, but\n          both work well alongside it if you're embedding the library\n          in a larger codebase.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          <strong>llms.txt</strong> &mdash; A short Markdown file at\n          your site root that summarizes the project for an LLM\n          fetching it. Recommended location: <code>/llms.txt</code>.\n          A reasonable template for projects using this library:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\"># My Watercolor App\n\nA &lt;one-sentence project description&gt;.\n\n## What it uses\n- Watercolor v0.29 library \u2014 a physical watercolor simulation\n  implementing Curtis et al. 1997. State is held inside a factory\n  instance returned by `Watercolor.create(targetEl, options)`.\n\n## API surface this app uses\n- wc.setBackground(name)\n- wc.paintAt(gx, gy, r, pigment, strength)\n- wc.exportPNG()\n\n## Where to look\n- src/main.js \u2014 instance creation + UI wiring\n- src/scenes/ \u2014 declarative scene definitions\n- public/index.html \u2014 markup the library mounts into\n\nSee the Watercolor library's own `/docs#agents` page for full\nAPI surface and gotchas.</pre>\n        <p>\n          <strong>AGENTS.md</strong> &mdash; A README-style file\n          aimed at coding agents. Whereas <code>README.md</code>\n          targets humans skim-reading on GitHub, <code>AGENTS.md</code>\n          targets an LLM looking for actionable knowledge. Keep it\n          terse, link to specific files, list constraints\n          explicitly.\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\"># AGENTS.md\n\n## Project shape\n- Single-page web app, no framework, vanilla JS.\n- Entry: `src/main.js` (creates one Watercolor instance).\n- No build step; deploy by uploading `index.html` + `src/`.\n\n## Conventions\n- Async: prefer `async/await`. No callbacks.\n- Naming: camelCase functions, PascalCase classes, ALL_CAPS constants.\n- File length cap: ~500 lines per file; split otherwise.\n\n## When making changes\n- DO NOT add npm dependencies without asking. This repo is\n  zero-dependency by policy.\n- DO NOT modify `vendor/watercolor-lib.js` \u2014 it's an external\n  library. To customize behavior, configure via options or wrap\n  the instance.\n- Tests live in `test/` and run via `node --test`.\n\n## Common tasks\n- Add a new scene: create `src/scenes/yourName.js` exporting a\n  default function `(wc) =&gt; void`, then register in `scenes.js`.\n- Change pigment palette: edit `PIGMENTS` array in\n  `vendor/watercolor-lib.js` \u2014 but consult `docs#agents` for the\n  K/S/density/granularity tuple meanings first.</pre>\n        <p>\n          <strong>For evaluations and benchmarks.</strong> If you're\n          building eval harnesses that drive this library, the\n          test pattern in\n          <a href=\"#agents-loop\">\"Driving from a loop\"</a> above\n          (Pattern 3) is the recommended shape: pointer-free\n          instance, sequence of programmatic paint calls, settle\n          period, capture frame, destroy.\n        </p>\n      </section>\n\n      <!-- ============================== CONCEPTS ============================== -->\n      <section class=\"docs-page\" data-section=\"concepts\">",

    "text": "**Added a full \"Agents & automation\" section to the docs.** Six subsections:\n\n**Quick spec** \u2014 A dense one-paragraph orientation for an agent landing cold. Explains what the library is, how to mount it, the coordinate system, and the idle behavior. The most important thing for an agent is knowing that coordinates are grid units (not pixels) and that the sim runs its own RAF loop so they don't need to step it manually.\n\n**Type signatures** \u2014 Every public method in TypeScript-style signatures. Includes the factory + options interface, all the brush state methods, painting/scenes/toggles/actions, plus pigment name resolution (`'rose' | 0`, `'cerulean' | 'blue' | 2`, etc.) and the exact error string thrown on bad names. This is the kind of thing an agent can pattern-match against without parsing the whole library.\n\n**Recipes** \u2014 Six worked code snippets: single dab, interpolated stroke, async paint-wait-paint-dry sequence, multi-instance, SVG trace with custom brush, frame capture for procedurally generating data, and listening for the lib's CustomEvents (`'rescaled'`, `'driedinstantly'`, `'pigmentchange'`).\n\n**Gotchas & invariants** \u2014 Nine non-obvious things that will save an agent from chasing phantom bugs:\n- Coordinates are grid units, not pixels\n- paintAt takes radius, not diameter\n- Pigments mix subtractively (Kubelka-Munk), not RGB-additively\n- `scale()` rebuilds the sim and clears the canvas\n- Mask is sticky until `removeMask()` or `reset()`\n- WebGL toggle changes frame rate, not physics\n- `fadePainting()` only fades painted pixels, not paper\n- `destroy()` is required for clean unmount\n- SVG `flipY` defaults to true\n- `pressure()` is captured at pointer-down and locked for the stroke\n\n**Driving from a loop** \u2014 Three concrete patterns for autonomous canvas driving: step-by-step deterministic sequence, continuous control loop with state polling, and a test-harness pattern using `{ pointer: false }` to prevent UI input from contaminating programmatic painting. Also calls out the determinism caveat (the sim isn't bit-deterministic across runs, so evals should compare structurally).\n\n**llms.txt & AGENTS.md** \u2014 Templates for the two emerging agent-readable file conventions, tuned to projects embedding this library. `llms.txt` is a project root summary fetched by an LLM with a URL; `AGENTS.md` is a README-style file aimed at coding agents working inside your repo. Both templates include do/don't constraints and specific file paths.\n\n**Sidebar nav** \u2014 New collapsible \"Agents & automation\" group between \"Programmatic API\" and \"Concepts\", with six jump-links to the subsections.\n\n**Changelog v0.29 entry** updated with a new bullet documenting the Agents section and the `{ pointer: false }` create option (which already existed but is now documented).\n\n**Verified:** both inline scripts (lib ~213KB, wiring ~21KB) parse cleanly after rebuild, total HTML is 377KB. The Agents section is positioned where it'll be discovered naturally by an agent crawling the docs in order (after the API reference) but also accessible directly via the sidebar jump links.",
